abstract: This study reconceptualizes influence operations (IOs) as a contemporary form of political brokerage, examining how covert campaigning enterprises orchestrate manipulative and inauthentic activities to secure electoral advantage. Using qualitative field research on the 2022 Philippine General Elections, the authors analyze how IOs operate as intermediaries between political clients and voters. The paper draws on 22 in-depth interviews with IO leads and staff to characterize the broker attributes, brokerage processes, and political-economic infrastructures that enable manipulation.
key_claims: Influence operations during elections function as a contemporary form of political brokerage rather than as isolated disinformation campaigns.
IOs possess distinctive broker attributes that allow them to mediate between political clients, digital platforms, and voting publics.
The manipulative capacity of IOs is sustained by political-economic infrastructures, not merely individual actors or content.
methods: Qualitative field research situated in the 2022 Philippine General Elections.
22 in-depth interviews with leads and staff working inside influence operations.
Conceptual analysis applying brokerage theory to the empirics of covert political campaigning.
findings: IOs exhibit specific broker attributes that position them as intermediaries in electoral politics.
Brokerage processes structure how IOs translate political demands into covert online campaign activities.
Political-economic infrastructures underpin and enable IO manipulation during Philippine elections.
contributions: Introduces a brokerage framework for theorizing influence operations in electoral contexts.
Provides empirical documentation of IO practices in the 2022 Philippine elections through insider interviews.
Shifts analytical attention from content-level disinformation to the political-economic infrastructures that sustain manipulation.
framing: The paper situates itself at the intersection of political communication, disinformation studies, and political sociology, extending classical brokerage theory to digital electoral manipulation. It contributes to scholarship on the Global South and Philippine politics by foregrounding the organizational and economic scaffolding of influence operations rather than treating them as ad hoc propaganda.
